senses.definition: plural of bronchostomy; surgical openings made into the bronchi (air passages in the lungs).
examples: The patient has had several bronchostomies to manage long-term airway problems.
Bronchostomies may be temporary or part of a longer treatment plan.

senses.definition: a surgical procedure that creates an opening into a bronchus to help with breathing or to drain material from the airways.
examples: A bronchostomy may be used when the normal airway route is blocked.
After the bronchostomy, doctors monitored the patient’s breathing closely.

senses.definition: a rare condition involving painful spasms (tightening) in the bronchial tubes.
examples: The symptoms were described as bronchotetany after further medical evaluation.
Bronchotetany is discussed in medical texts as a very uncommon respiratory spasm.

senses.definition: an (archaic) surgical instrument used for cutting into the bronchi.
examples: In older medical descriptions, a bronchotome is mentioned as a cutting instrument for the bronchi.
The term bronchotome appears in historical surgical vocabulary.

senses.definition: an (archaic) surgeon who performs bronchotomy procedures.
examples: The historical record refers to a bronchotomist as a specialist in airway surgery.
The term bronchotomist is rarely used outside old medical writing.

senses.definition: a surgical procedure (or incision) made into a bronchus (the airway tubes leading into the lungs).
examples: Bronchotomy may be performed to gain access to the bronchial airway.
After bronchotomy, careful monitoring is needed to prevent complications.

senses.definition: relating to both the bronchi and the trachea (windpipe), or to an airway connection between them.
examples: The doctor mentioned a bronchotracheal connection as part of the patient’s condition.
A bronchotracheal injury can affect airflow through the airway.

senses.definition: an (older/rare) term for a type of illness involving the bronchi, described as similar to typhoid.
examples: The case notes use the term bronchotyphoid in older medical language.
Bronchotyphoid is mentioned in historical descriptions of respiratory infections.

senses.definition: an (older/rare) term for an illness affecting the bronchi that was described in relation to typhus.
examples: Bronchotyphus appears as a historical term in older medical texts.
The symptoms were recorded under the diagnosis bronchotyphus.

senses.definition: relating to the bronchi and the lung alveoli (small air sacs).
examples: Bronchovesicular sounds are described during a chest examination.
The report includes bronchovesicular breath sounds.

senses.definition: one of the main air passages in the lungs that carries air from the trachea to the smaller airways.
examples: The bronchus branches into smaller airways.
Doctors listened for abnormal sounds in the bronchus area.

senses.definition: a wild or half-wild horse, often used in rodeos.
examples: He tried to ride a bronco at the rodeo.
The bronco bucked and threw the rider.

senses.definition: a rodeo rider who attempts to stay on a bucking bronco (often used as a colorful or informal term).
examples: The crowd cheered for the broncobuster as he held on.
He became known as a broncobuster after winning multiple rodeo events.

senses.definition: plural of broncobuster; rodeo riders who try to stay on bucking broncos.
examples: Broncobusters competed for the top prize this weekend.
The lineup included many broncobusters from different states.

senses.definition: the activity of riding or trying to ride a bucking bronco, especially in rodeos.
examples: Broncobusting is dangerous, so riders wear protective gear.
He practiced broncobusting every day to improve his balance.

senses.definition: plural of bronco; wild or half-wild horses, especially ones used in rodeos.
examples: The rodeo featured several broncos.
The broncos bucked hard during the competition.

senses.definition: not a standard English word; likely a spelling/typing variant of “broncos,” meaning wild or half-wild rodeo horses.
examples: The document clearly meant “broncos” but spelled it as “bronc s.”
In casual notes, people sometimes write “bronc s” by accident.

senses.definition: not a common contemporary English word; likely a misspelling or variant of another term depending on context.
examples: I think “brond” is a typo for a different word in this sentence.
Without context, it’s hard to give a reliable meaning for “brond.”

senses.definition: not a standard contemporary English word; likely a misspelling or variant of another term depending on context.
examples: The author seems to have meant a different word than “bronds.”
In this context, “bronds” doesn’t match common English usage.

senses.definition: unclear as a standard English word; possibly a proper name, product name, or a highly specialized/rare term.
examples: “Brondyron” appeared as a name in the document.
I’m not sure of the meaning of “brondyron” without more context.

senses.definition: unclear as a standard English word; likely a plural form of “brondyron” used as a name or label in a specific context.
examples: The website lists several “brondyrons.”
These “brondyrons” are mentioned as a group in the text.

senses.definition: a rare term used in geology/mineralogy to name a specific mineral (specialized technical usage).
examples: The specimen was identified as brongniardite by its crystal properties.
Brongniardite is discussed in mineralogical catalogs.

senses.definition: not a common contemporary English word; it may be a misspelling or a very rare/technical term depending on context.
examples: I suspect “bronk” is a typo here.
Can you share the sentence where “bronk” is used?

senses.definition: unclear as a standard English word; likely a misspelling/variant of another term depending on context.
examples: “Bronstrops” doesn’t look like standard English in this sentence.
The word might be a spelling error for something else.

senses.definition: unclear as a standard English word; likely related to the Brontë family (e.g., a name used for works or collections), depending on context.
examples: The article referenced bronteana as a set of Brontë-related writings.
I think bronteana is a label for Brontë material in that publication.
